title: Ben Lerner’s <cite>Transcription</cite> Is a Brilliant Meditation on Tech
summary: Authenticity, performance, the thin line between fact and fiction: these are, by now, the well-known central preoccupations of Ben Lerner’s fiction. His first novel, Leaving the Atocha Station, follows an American poet named Adam Gordon in Madrid in 2004, who witnesses the aftermath of the 11-M commuter train bombings. Adam is a divisive lead; he’s […]
reason: A literary review of a novel about authenticity and performance is primarily representational content analysis, not a framework-illuminating story about capture mechanisms or commons dynamics.

title: Americans (Still) Support a Federal Jobs Guarantee
summary: In 2024, the Center for Working-Class Politics (CWCP) and Jacobin published a report summarizing the state of public opinion on a federal jobs guarantee. Looking across nine publicly available polls conducted since 2018, we found an average of 59% support for the policy. That’s not a slim majority driven by one partisan base but a […]
reason: This is a polling summary about representational support for a policy — content-level data about what people say they want, not evidence of routing-level change or enclosure dynamics.

title: A Socialist Teacher Is Running for Kentucky State House

title: When Rank-and-File Unionists Took On the Mob
summary: My first contact with labor reformers in New York City was nearly fifty years ago. Like many rank-and-file dissidents before and since, these critics of union corruption were prophets without honor in their own union local. Teamsters Local 282 was at the time one of the most mobbed-up affiliates of a national union, then rightly […]
reason: This documents rank-and-file workers building internal democratic circuits against both mob capture and corrupt leadership — a concrete historical example of the commune layer defending itself from enclosure within an institution nominally built to protect it.
angle: Use the routing architecture tool: the mob-union relationship was a capture mechanism that rerouted worker collective power into private extraction, and rank-and-file reform movements were attempts to rebuild flows of solidarity and accountability through horizontal rather than hierarchical channels.

title: Europe’s Steel Industry Should Be Publicly Owned and Controlled
summary: Europe’s steel sector is in crisis. The obvious culprit is the continent’s high energy prices, now further escalating in the wake of the Iran war. But the deeper problem lies elsewhere: the industry risks returning increasingly fewer profits to company bosses and shareholders. Amid their troubles, there’s an opportunity for the Left to make a […]

title: New York Is Closing In on Amazon’s Shady Delivery System
summary: Amazon delivery drivers in New York wear the company’s uniforms, follow its routes, and are tracked by its software. Yet, legally, they don’t work for Amazon. The Delivery Protection Act (DPA), a bill introduced by socialist New York City Councilor Tiffany Cabán and with a committee hearing scheduled for April 9, would try to resolve […]
reason: This story shows a classic enclosure mechanism — the fissured workplace model — where Amazon captures labor value while legally routing liability away from itself, and a legislative attempt to reroute accountability back through the common.
angle: Use the routing architecture tool: Amazon's contractor system is not primarily about employment classification but about engineering a flow that extracts labor value while routing risk, cost, and legal accountability onto workers and shell companies — the DPA attempts a counter-routing.

title: Israel Can’t Even Tolerate a West Bank Football Pitch

title: Toronto’s Transit Crisis Is a Class Crisis
summary: Toronto households spent more than CA$440 million on ride-hailing services like Uber and Lyft in 2023 — just over half of what they spent on public transit. title: The Federal Government Is Rushing Toward AI. Our Reporting Offers Three Cautionary Tales.
summary: The post The Federal Government Is Rushing Toward AI. Our Reporting Offers Three Cautionary Tales. appeared first on ProPublica .
reason: Federal AI adoption illustrates the epistemic incompetence of centralization — algorithmic systems destroying distributed intelligence needed to administer complex social programs — at unprecedented scale.
angle: Use the 'epistemic incompetence of centralization' tool to show how replacing caseworker judgment with centralized AI models systematically destroys the local, relational knowledge required to actually serve people.

title: Lack of Democracy in United Farm Workers Gave Chavez Immunity
summary: In 2011 Frank Bardacke published an 800-page history of the Farm Workers union: Trampling Out the Vintage: Cesar Chavez and the Two Souls of the United Farm Workers . It opened many eyes to the reasons the UFW became a shadow of its former self. Bardacke starts the book with an epigraph, a quote from Shakespeare’s Julius Caesar: “O what a fall was there, my countrymen! Then I, and you, and all of us fell down...”

title: Fortress Yellowstone
